analysis.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition

bottleneck.description: Every one of the 20 cuBLAS kernel launches across all 9 aten::mm operators shows smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active = 0.0. The model runs FP32 GEMMs through the cuBLAS 'Kernel2' SIMT SGEMM path, bypassing Blackwell HMMA Tensor Cores entirely. GEMM kernels consume 1,759,670 ns of the 1,788,086 ns total (98.4%), yet Tensor Cores — which on Blackwell deliver ~8x the BF16 TFLOPS of FP32 SIMT — are completely idle. The kernel also runs at 200-210 registers/thread, capping warp occupancy at 8-17% and preventing memory latency hiding. On the 2048x2048 projections (op_id=5,7,15,17), sm__throughput is 23% and eligible_cycles_pct is 36%, both consistent with a compute-bound SIMT kernel starved of warp slots. On the 2048x512 projections (op_id=9,19), sm__throughput drops to 6.2% and eligible_cycles_pct to 12%, consistent with wave starvation on top of the SIMT bottleneck.

transformation.description: Cast the entire model to BF16 before torch.compile. This forces cuBLAS to dispatch the HMMA Tensor Core path for every aten::mm and aten::addmm node. The cuBLAS SIMT 'Kernel2' is replaced by a TC-native kernel with far lower register pressure (~32-64 regs/thread vs 200-210), recovering warp occupancy and engaging HMMA instructions. The fused Triton activation kernels (triton_poi_fused_addmm_relu_0, triton_poi_fused_addmm_gelu_1, triton_poi_fused_addmm_silu_2, triton_poi_fused_addmm_tanh_3) will also operate natively in BF16, eliminating any need for in-kernel FP32 upcasting. If whole-model BF16 is unacceptable due to accuracy constraints, the FX surgery approach inserts aten.to.dtype casts at each mm boundary.
transformation.location: All torch.ops.aten.mm.default and torch.ops.aten.addmm.default call_function nodes in the Inductor FX graph
fx_steps: # --- Selective FX surgery alternative (keeps model FP32 externally) ---
nodes_to_cast = [n for n in gm.graph.nodes if n.op == 'call_function' and n.target in GEMM_OPS]
node.args = tuple(gm.graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.to.dtype, args=(a, torch.bfloat16)) if isinstance(a, fx.Node) else a for a in node.args)

bottleneck.description: After BF16 is applied (OPT-1), the two output-projection GEMMs [256x2048 @ 2048x512] and two input-projection GEMMs [256x512 @ 512x2048] remain wave-starved due to small M=256. The 2048x512 output projections currently launch grid (8,1,22)=176 thread blocks on 84 SMs, giving 2.1 blocks/SM average occupancy. eligible_cycles_pct=12.06% on kernel k_00045 (dominant for op_id=9) confirms the warp scheduler is stalled 88% of cycles, consistent with fewer active warps than needed to hide memory and arithmetic latency. The default cuBLAS heuristic selects a fixed split-K factor of 22 for K=2048 that does not saturate the SM array. The Inductor max_autotune search covers Triton configs including variable split-K, tile sizes (e.g. 64x64, 128x128), and warp counts, and can find significantly more wave-covering configurations than the cuBLAS default for these non-square shapes.

transformation.description: Enable Inductor's exhaustive GEMM autotuning. This causes Inductor to benchmark multiple Triton kernel configurations for each unique GEMM shape before selecting the fastest one. For [256x2048 @ 2048x512], a tuned Triton kernel with split-K=8 or 16 would create 256-512+ blocks vs the current 176, covering the 84-SM Blackwell array significantly better. For [256x512 @ 512x2048] (op_id=3,13, 40K ns each), a 64x64 or 128x64 Triton tile also improves over the current (16,2,6)=192 block dispatch. The search runs once at compile time and is cached.
transformation.location: torch.compile call site, or torch._inductor.config before compilation

bottleneck.description: This is an alternative path to Tensor Core engagement for teams that cannot adopt BF16 (e.g., due to FP32 accumulation requirements). On PyTorch 2.x, torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 defaults to True, but the measured tensor_core_active_pct = 0.0 on all GEMMs indicates TF32 is either explicitly disabled in the workload or a cuBLAS heuristic is bypassing the TC path for these specific shapes. The four 2048x2048 GEMMs (op_id=5,7,15,17) each consume ~101K ns with sm_throughput 23% — far below TF32's theoretical ceiling. Explicitly setting allow_tf32=True and forcing cuBLAS workspace allocation can unblock the TC heuristic for these shapes without any dtype change to the model.

transformation.description: Explicitly enable TF32 Tensor Core dispatch for FP32 matrix multiplications. TF32 rounds FP32 mantissa to 10 bits internally but maintains FP32 exponent range and accumulates in FP32, making it numerically safe for most inference workloads. For the four 2048x2048 GEMMs and two 512x2048 GEMMs, this routes cuBLAS through the HMMA path at reduced precision without changing the model's external API dtype. Also allocate a cuBLAS workspace to ensure the TC-enabled algorithm variants are considered during kernel selection. This optimization is ALTERNATIVE to OPT-1 — apply one or the other, not both.
transformation.location: Model initialization or inference entry point, before any torch.compile call
fx_steps: # Set TF32 flags before model compilation
# Allocate cuBLAS workspace to unlock TC algorithm search
# Verify TC engagement post-hoc: run ncu and check smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active > 0

bottleneck.description: With compile_mode='inductor' (no CUDA graph capture), every forward pass incurs CPU-side kernel dispatch overhead for each of the approximately 12 GPU kernel launches (4 cuBLAS mm calls + 4 fused Triton activation kernels + misc). On a modern system, each cuLaunchKernel call adds ~5-10 microseconds of CPU overhead. At 12 launches per forward pass, this is 60-120 us of CPU overhead that serializes launch scheduling and can become a bottleneck at high inference throughput (small batch, low GPU latency per pass). Under ncu replay the GPU durations are inflated 2-5x, so the CPU overhead fraction is understated in the profile; in real execution at ~600 us total GPU time, 60-120 us CPU overhead represents 10-20% overhead for tight-loop inference.

transformation.description: Capture the entire forward pass as a CUDA graph using torch.compile with mode='reduce-overhead'. This replays the full 12-kernel sequence from a single CPU-side graph launch, eliminating individual cuLaunchKernel overhead. The model is statically shaped (batch=256, all dims fixed), making it a direct candidate for CUDA graph capture. The Inductor 'reduce-overhead' mode automates graph capture and replay, requiring no manual CUDAGraph API calls.
fx_steps: # Replace the existing torch.compile call with reduce-overhead mode
# Warm up to trigger graph capture (at least 3 identical-shape forward passes)
# Subsequent calls replay the CUDA graph with near-zero CPU overhead

bottleneck.description: The two output-projection GEMMs [256x2048] @ [2048x512] (op_id=9 k_00045, op_id=19 k_00053) dispatch grid (8,1,22)=176 thread blocks to 84 SMs, yielding 2.1 blocks/SM average. With each block holding 128 threads (4 warps), and register pressure capping SM occupancy at ~10 warps, only ~2.5 blocks/SM can be resident simultaneously. eligible_cycles_pct=12.03-12.06% confirms the warp scheduler has almost no eligible warps 88% of cycles — there is no parallelism to hide latency. The root cause is M=256 being too small relative to the chosen tile: the (8,1) M-N tiling partitions the [256x512] output into 8 M-tiles of 32 rows each with only one N-tile covering all 512 columns, leaving the split-K dimension (22) as the only source of parallelism. Padding M from 256 to a multiple of 128 increases output tiles proportionally.

transformation.description: Pad the batch dimension from 256 to 384 (the next multiple of 128 that improves SM coverage) immediately before each [256x2048] @ [2048x512] mm node, then slice the output back to [256x512]. With M=384, the cuBLAS grid becomes (12,1,22)=264 blocks, raising SM coverage from 2.1 to 3.1 blocks/SM. The extra 128 rows of compute are zero-padded and masked in the output slice. Net arithmetic overhead is +50% for these two GEMMs only, but the wave coverage improvement reduces effective latency by ~25-30%. This transformation is most valuable in inference serving where batch size is fixed and per-layer latency dominates. Note: this optimization is independent of OPT-1 but the absolute gains are larger after BF16 is applied because TC kernel tiling is finer-grained than SIMT tiling.
transformation.location: torch.ops.aten.mm.default nodes for op_id=9 and op_id=19 in the Inductor FX graph
fx_steps: TARGET_SHAPES = {(256, 2048): (2048, 512)}  # input_shape -> weight_shape for output projections
def pad_output_projection_m(gm: fx.GraphModule, pad_to: int = 384) -> fx.GraphModule:
if not (hasattr(a, 'meta') and a.meta.get('tensor_meta') is not None):

transformation.code_hint: aten.constant_pad_nd(x, [0,0,0,128], 0.0) before mm; aten.slice(out, 0, 0, 256) after mm for op_id=9 and op_id=19 nodes
transformation.notes: This transform requires shape metadata (node.meta['tensor_meta']) to be populated, which is available only after Inductor's shape propagation pass. It must be applied as a post-lowering FX pass, not a pre-dispatch pass. Verify correctness by checking that output[:256, :] == reference_output[:256, :] within floating point tolerance.

global_notes: EDGE CASE FLAG — ncu_replay_timing: All duration_ns values in this profile are 2-5x longer than real GPU execution time because ncu application-mode replay runs the full workload multiple times per counter group. Relative percentages between operators are accurate; absolute ns values are not representative of production latency.

WORKLOAD COMPOSITION: 1,759,670 ns of the 1,788,086 ns total attributed time (98.4%) is consumed by cuBLAS 'Kernel2' SIMT SGEMM kernels. The fused Triton activation kernels (triton_poi_fused_addmm_relu_0/gelu_1/silu_2/tanh_3) consume only 28,416 ns (1.6%) and are already near-optimally implemented — they run at 28-30% DRAM throughput and 65-75% warp occupancy on the ReLU/GELU/SiLU paths. Optimization effort should be entirely directed at the GEMM path.

DUAL ATTRIBUTION: The profile contains two attribution contexts — (1) aten::mm_0 and layer::unique::prologue_0 with kernel IDs k_00015 through k_00038 (high/medium confidence, torch_profiler and nvtx attribution from a prologue measurement pass), and (2) individually-named aten::mm op_id=3 through op_id=19 with kernel IDs k_00039 through k_00053 (nvtx attribution from a second pass). These represent separate measurement iterations and are not double-counted in the total_attributed_ns sum. The per-shape GEMM characteristics are consistent across both passes.

OPT-1 vs OPT-3 are ALTERNATIVES: Apply OPT-1 (BF16) for maximum throughput gain (~49% reduction). Apply OPT-3 (TF32 enable) as a less invasive alternative that preserves FP32 external API while still engaging Tensor Cores (~35% reduction). Do not stack both — once TC is engaged via either path, the other provides no additional gain.

OPT-2 DEPENDENCY: OPT-2 (max_autotune) should be applied at the same dtype as OPT-1. Autotuning at FP32 will find different (and suboptimal) Triton configs compared to autotuning at BF16. Apply OPT-1 first, then OPT-2 in a single compile invocation via mode='max-autotune'.

TANH KERNEL EFFICIENCY: triton_poi_fused_addmm_tanh_3 shows eligible_cycles_pct=6% and sm_throughput_pct=2%, but this operator (aten::addmm_0, duration 11,392 ns) is only 0.64% of total time and falls below the 1% optimization threshold. The low efficiency is intrinsic to the tiny output tensor [256x512] = 131,072 elements and is not actionable without changing the model architecture.

DEVICE CONTEXT: warp_cycles_per_instruction is null for all operators (removed on Blackwell). Latency-bound classification uses eligible_cycles_pct < 20 as the indicator. The output-projection GEMMs (eligible_cycles_pct=12%) and the tanh activation kernel (eligible_cycles_pct=6%) are both latency-bound by this criterion.

BLACKWELL ARCHITECTURE NOTE: RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (GB202) has approximately 84 SMs. Waves/SM for the current dispatch: 2048x2048 GEMMs dispatch ~176 blocks (2.1 waves), 2048x512 projections dispatch 176 blocks (2.1 waves), 512x2048 projections dispatch 192 blocks (2.3 waves). All shapes are sub-4 waves, meaning each SM completes its assigned blocks and sits idle waiting for the next synchronization. After BF16 switch (OPT-1), TC kernels use finer tile sizes (typically 16x16 MMA tiles) which may increase block count and improve wave coverage organically.
